skill.name: skills-cli
skill.description: Guides agents through discovering, installing, listing, updating, removing, backing up, and restoring Agent Skills with the skills CLI.
skill.summary: Manage Agent Skills with CLI discovery, installation, maintenance, and restore workflows.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command and URL patterns, but review shows they are Markdown examples and workflow instructions, not executable code in the skill package. The remaining risk is legitimate but material: the skill instructs agents to run external package-manager commands that can fetch remote repositories and modify local skill installations.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands Can Modify Skill Installations
medium_findings.description: The skill intentionally tells agents to run bunx skills and bunx skvlt commands for installing, updating, removing, syncing, backing up, and restoring skills. This is a legitimate purpose, but it can change the filesystem and installed agent behavior, especially when combined with global, all-skill, or yes-to-prompts flags.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit Markdown examples for package management operations. They are not hidden execution code, but their documented use clearly changes local installations.

medium_findings.title: Remote Skill Sources Require Trust Review
medium_findings.description: The installation examples accept GitHub, GitLab, git, local path, and marketplace sources. Remote skill sources are expected for this tool, but agents should verify source reputation before installing because skills can influence future agent behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents remote installation sources and also includes quality-check guidance. This supports a medium-risk, legitimate-use assessment.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation Links
low_findings.description: The URL detections point to skills.sh, GitHub, and GitLab examples used for discovery, installation, and documentation. No evidence found that the skill automatically contacts these endpoints or exfiltrates data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Each URL appears in prose, tables, or command examples. There is no script or executable network client in the package.

low_findings.title: Screen Capture Upload Static Hit Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The phrase on the flagged line describes a recommended screenshot skill example. No upload behavior, file transfer code, or screenshot collection logic appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context is a plain text recommendation example. The static finding was triggered by wording, not behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged weak cryptography lines contain ordinary skill-management text and category examples. No evidence found of cryptographic algorithms, hashing routines, encryption code, or security-sensitive crypto choices.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no crypto-related implementation. The hits appear to be keyword collisions in Markdown content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Manager Command Instructions
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many bunx command examples. These are expected for a CLI management skill, but they should be run only with trusted sources and user consent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are plainly documented and not obfuscated. The risk comes from their intended operational effect, not from hidden code execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Repository Installation Sources
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents remote repository sources for installation. This creates supply-chain exposure if an agent installs from an untrusted repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Remote repository examples are explicit and central to the workflow. The file also recommends source reputation checks, reducing but not removing risk.

content.user_title: Manage Agent Skills from the CLI
content.value_statement: Finding and maintaining Agent Skills can be inconsistent across tools and machines. This skill gives agents a clear CLI workflow for discovery, installation, updates, removal, backup, and restore.
actual_capabilities: Guides discovery of Agent Skills through skills.sh and CLI search.
Provides install patterns for single, multiple, global, and agent-specific skills.
Explains listing, checking, updating, removing, restoring, and syncing installed skills.
Distinguishes symlink and copy installation methods for different environments.
Recommends source reputation checks before installing community skills.
Introduces Skills Vault for portable backup and restore workflows.
limitations: It does not implement the CLI itself or run commands automatically.
It depends on the current behavior of external tools such as bunx, npx, and skills.sh.
It cannot guarantee that third-party skill sources are safe or maintained.
It is not a skill-authoring guide for writing SKILL.md content.

use_cases.title: Find a Relevant Skill
use_cases.description: A developer asks for a reusable workflow and receives search guidance, quality checks, and an installable recommendation.
use_cases.target_user: Developers extending an agent with specialized workflows

use_cases.title: Install Skills for One Agent
use_cases.description: A team member installs a selected skill for Codex, Claude, or another supported agent with the correct scope and flags.
use_cases.target_user: AI tool users managing local agent capabilities

use_cases.title: Rebuild a Skill Setup
use_cases.description: An operations user backs up, restores, or syncs installed skills across machines using lock files or Skills Vault guidance.
use_cases.target_user: DevOps and platform teams standardizing agent environments

prompt_templates.title: Find a Skill
prompt_templates.prompt: Find an Agent Skill for my task. My task is: [describe task]. Check trust signals before recommending one.

prompt_templates.title: Install for an Agent
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me install [skill name] for [agent name]. Use the safest default scope unless I specify otherwise.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Installed Skills
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my installed Agent Skills workflow. Show how to list skills, check updates, and remove unused entries.

prompt_templates.title: Standardize Team Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repeatable Agent Skills setup for a team. Include discovery, source review, installation scope, backup, restore, and update practices.

output_examples.input: Find a skill for pull request reviews.
output_examples.output: A concise recommendation with the skill name, source reputation, install count, why it matches, and a suggested installation step.

output_examples.input: Install this skill for Codex only.
output_examples.output: A scoped installation plan that targets Codex, explains default symlink behavior, and asks for confirmation before changing local setup.

output_examples.input: Move my skills to a new laptop.
output_examples.output: A restore workflow that compares lock-file restoration with Skills Vault, then recommends the portable manifest path when sharing across machines.

best_practices: Check source reputation, install volume, and repository quality before installing community skills.
Use agent-specific and project-specific scopes when broad global installation is not required.
Prefer dry-run or preview workflows before restoring or changing many installed skills.
anti_patterns: Do not install a search result without checking whether the source is established.
Do not use broad all-skill or global flags when the user asked for one agent or one project.
Do not assume no skill exists after one vague search term; try specific adjacent terms first.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps agents use the skills CLI to discover, install, list, update, remove, back up, restore, and sync Agent Skills.

faq.question: Does this skill install anything by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides workflow guidance and command patterns. The agent or user still decides whether to run commands.

faq.question: Which tools can use the guidance?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Why is source review important?
faq.answer: Installed skills can affect future agent behavior. Trusted publishers and maintained repositories reduce supply-chain risk.

faq.question: When should I use copy instead of symlink?
faq.answer: Use copy mode when symlinks are unsupported, inconvenient, or when portable independent files are required.

faq.question: Is this the right skill for writing a new skill?
faq.answer: No. It is for managing skills with the CLI. Use a skill-authoring workflow to write or improve SKILL.md content.
